sections.title: Findings
sections.content: Real app logic lives almost entirely in `apps/crawler` (Python). `apps/web` is still the stock TanStack Start template — `todos` router is a hardcoded array, `table.tsx`/`chat-area.tsx` are the framework's own demo code, schema is just a `todos` table. Nothing there yet exhibits N+1/re-render/caching problems because there's no real data layer wired up. Findings below are all from the crawler.

sections.title: 1. Redundant O(n log n) resort on every single save (`base.py:283-314`)
sections.content:
`crawl()` calls `_save_state` after **every** article (even skipped non-articles), and each call does `sorted(seen)` over the *entire* seen-URLs set. For an unlimited crawl (`--limit 0`, meant for full-archive runs) that's an O(n² log n) sort cost plus a full JSON rewrite per URL — pure waste, since `seen_urls` doesn't need to be sorted for correctness (it's only ever loaded back into a `set`).

```python

sections.title: before: rebuilds + sorts the whole set every save
after: append-only, no resort
sections.content:
seen_urls: list[str] = state.setdefault("seen_urls", list(seen))
...
seen.add(url)
seen_urls.append(url)
```
`→ skipped: batching disk writes (would weaken the "lose at most 1 article on crash" guarantee), add if full-archive runs start hitting real latency.`

sections.title: 2. Unbounded memory growth in `crawl()`'s return value (`base.py:284,309,335`)
sections.content:
`saved: list[Article]` accumulates full `Article` objects — body text + image URL list — for the whole run, even though each one is already flushed to JSONL incrementally. The only caller (`main.py:25`) just does `len(articles)`. On `--limit 0` over a large site this holds every article body in RAM for no reason.

```python

sections.title: base.py
sections.content:
def crawl(self, limit: int = 10, on_save=None) -> int:
    ...
    saved_count = 0
    ...
    saved_count += 1
    self._append_article(article)
    ...
    if limit > 0 and saved_count >= limit:
        ...
        return saved_count
    ...
    return saved_count

sections.title: main.py
sections.content:
count = source.crawl(limit=args.limit, on_save=...)
print(f"got {count} new articles this run")
```

sections.title: 3. Fully serial fetching, no concurrency
sections.content:
Every provider fetches one article at a time via synchronous `Fetcher.get`/`StealthyFetcher.fetch` — wall-clock time is 100% network-bound and fully serialized. `Moov` and `FreeNews` already document (in-code) that they double-fetch some pages, which is a real, larger caching win, but that ceiling is already ponytail-flagged in the source and left as-is deliberately.
`→ skipped here: parallelizing fetches (needs per-site rate-limit tuning + thread safety on state.json), add if crawl runtime becomes the bottleneck.`

sections.title: Not real findings (verified before writing this)
sections.content:
- No N+1 SQL pattern exists — `apps/web`'s db layer (Drizzle + `todos` table) isn't queried in a loop anywhere yet.
- No React re-render issues in custom code — the only components using hooks are shadcn/ui primitives and TanStack's own demo routes (`table.tsx`, `chat-area.tsx`), already optimized upstream (`useMemo` on columns, debounced input, etc.).
- No caching gap — there's no repeated-fetch-of-same-resource pattern outside the two already-documented ceilings above.

One line each — the two disk-I/O fixes (#1, #2) are safe, no-tradeoff wins; #3 is a real lever but changes crash-recovery semantics, worth a deliberate decision rather than a silent patch. Say if you want #1/#2 applied now.

codeBlocks.code:

# after: append-only, no resort

...
seen.add(url)
seen_urls.append(url)

    ...

    ...

    self._append_article(article)
    ...

        ...
        return saved_count
    ...
    return saved_count

# main.py

print(f"got {count} new articles this run")

rawOutputPreview:
## Findings

Real app logic lives almost entirely in `apps/crawler` (Python). `apps/web` is still the stock TanStack Start template — `todos` router is a hardcoded array, `table.tsx`/`chat-area.tsx` are the framework's own demo code, schema is just a `todos` table. Nothing there yet exhibits N+1/re-render/caching problems because there's no real data layer wired up. Findings below are all from the crawler.

### 1. Redundant O(n log n) resort on every single save (`base.py:283-314`)

`crawl()` calls `_save_state` after **every** article (even skipped non-articles), and each call does `sorted(seen)` over the *entire* seen-URLs set. For an unlimited crawl (`--limit 0`, meant for full-archive runs) that's an O(n² log n) sort cost plus a full JSON rewrite per URL — pure waste, since `seen_urls` doesn't need to be sorted for correctness (it's only ever loaded back into a `set`).

```python
# before: rebuilds + sorts the whole set every save
state["seen_urls"] = sorted(seen)

# after: append-only, no resort
seen_urls: list[str] = state.setdefault("seen_urls", list(seen))
...
seen.add(url)
seen_urls.append(url)
```
`→ skipped: batching disk writes (would weaken the "lose at most 1 article on crash" guarantee), add if full-archive runs start hitting real latency.`

### 2. Unbounded memory growth in `crawl()`'s return value (`base.py:284,309,335`)

`saved: list[Article]` accumulates full `Article` objects — body text + image URL list — for the whole run, even though each one is already flushed to JSONL incrementally. The only caller (`main.py:25`) just does `len(articles)`. On `--limit 0` over a large site this holds every article body in RAM for no reason.

```python
# base.py
def crawl(self, limit: int = 10, on_save=None) -> int:
    ...
    saved_count = 0
    ...
    saved_count += 1
    self._append_article(article)
    ...
    if limit > 0 and saved_count >= limit:
        ...
        return saved_count
    ...
    return saved_count
